skill.name: requesting-code-review
skill.description: Guides AI assistants to request structured code reviews after implementation work and before merging changes.
skill.summary: Request structured code review for completed changes before merge.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command and blocker patterns, but most are markdown examples or plain text false positives. The confirmed risk is that the skill asks an agent to run git shell commands with placeholder commit ranges, which is legitimate for code review but should use trusted SHAs or refs.

medium_findings.title: Git Shell Commands Use Placeholder Refs
medium_findings.description: The review template instructs the agent to run git diff commands with {BASE_SHA} and {HEAD_SHA}. This supports normal code review, but it is risky if arbitrary user-supplied values are executed in a shell instead of trusted commit SHAs or refs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in a bash code block and directly include placeholder values. The intended use is legitimate git review, so the risk depends on whether callers validate the placeholder values.

medium_findings.title: Command Substitution Examples Run Local Git Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill shows command substitution for git rev-parse and git log pipelines to compute review ranges. These are ordinary local repository commands, but they still require shell execution and should be run only in trusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly execute local git commands through shell syntax. No network access or secret access is shown, so this is a moderate operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography locations are ordinary text such as description, design, and DESCRIPTION placeholders. No cryptographic algorithm, hash selection, encryption code, or credential handling is present at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain normal prose or placeholder names, not cryptographic code. This is a strong false positive from substring matching.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance locations are review guidance and sample issue text, not commands that collect host, network, user, or process information. No evidence found of system reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are checklist or example-review content only. They do not instruct collection of system details or access sensitive operating system data.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Triggered Execution Heuristics
low_findings.description: Several Ruby backtick and template-literal findings correspond to markdown fences and examples. The files are documentation and prompt templates, not Ruby, JavaScript, or executable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backtick patterns occur in markdown code fences. Some fenced commands are real git instructions, but the Ruby and template-literal classifications are not semantically accurate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Git Diff With Placeholder Range
dangerous_patterns.description: The review prompt includes shell commands that interpolate placeholder base and head refs into a git diff range. Use only validated SHAs or trusted branch refs when following this template.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a concrete shell command pattern with placeholder interpolation. It is useful for review but can become unsafe if untrusted values are copied into a shell.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution For Review SHAs
dangerous_patterns.description: The usage guide demonstrates command substitution to populate BASE_SHA and HEAD_SHA from git commands. This is expected workflow automation but remains external command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is explicit and local to git. The absence of network calls or secret reads lowers severity, but users should still run it in trusted repositories.

content.value_statement: Code changes can miss requirements, edge cases, or production risks before merge. This skill provides a structured review workflow for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Provides a repeatable workflow for requesting review after implementation tasks.
Supplies a code-reviewer prompt template with required implementation context.
Guides reviewers to compare changes against requirements and plans.
Organizes findings into critical, important, and minor severity levels.
Prompts a clear merge-readiness assessment with reasoning.
Encourages developers to fix high-impact issues before continuing.
limitations: Requires access to the relevant git history and code changes.
Does not run tests or verify behavior by itself.
Depends on accurate requirements and implementation summaries from the user.
May need manual validation for security-sensitive or production-critical changes.

use_cases.title: Review Feature Work Before Merge
use_cases.description: Check a completed feature against its requirements before merging to the main branch.

use_cases.title: Validate Agent-Generated Changes
use_cases.description: Ask a separate reviewer to inspect code produced during an AI-assisted development task.

use_cases.title: Create Consistent Review Gates
use_cases.description: Use the same review structure across implementation batches, refactors, and bug fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Review Request
prompt_templates.scenario: Use after a small completed change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my latest code change for correctness, missed requirements, and obvious bugs. Categorize findings by severity and give a merge-readiness verdict.

prompt_templates.title: Review Against Requirements
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when a plan or ticket defines expected behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the changes from BASE_SHA to HEAD_SHA against this requirement summary: [paste requirements]. Focus on gaps, regressions, edge cases, and test coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Production Readiness Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use before merging a major feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a production-readiness review of this implementation. Check architecture, error handling, data safety, migrations, backward compatibility, tests, and documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Diff Review Gate
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for multi-task development or release preparation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the git range from the trusted base ref to the trusted head ref. Compare the diff with the plan, list file-level issues, separate critical from important issues, and recommend merge, merge with fixes, or do not merge.

output_examples.input: A developer requests review after completing a new validation function.
output_examples.output: The reviewer highlights strong test coverage, flags one missing edge case, recommends adding a user-facing error, and marks the change ready with fixes.

output_examples.input: A team lead asks for a release-gate review before merging a refactor.
output_examples.output: The reviewer confirms the design is simpler, identifies a backward compatibility risk, and recommends delaying merge until migration behavior is documented.

output_examples.input: An AI-assisted developer wants a second pass on generated code.
output_examples.output: The reviewer compares the change against the plan, separates real defects from style comments, and gives a clear verdict on whether to continue.

best_practices: Use trusted commit SHAs or branch refs when preparing a review range.
Provide the reviewer with the plan, requirements, and implementation summary.
Fix critical and important findings before starting the next development task.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for review without giving the reviewer the relevant requirements.
Do not treat minor style feedback as equal to production-blocking defects.
Do not execute copied shell commands with untrusted placeholder values.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps request structured code review after implementation work and before merging changes.

faq.question: Does it perform the review automatically?
faq.answer: It provides the review workflow and prompt template. The assistant or reviewer still analyzes the code changes.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What inputs should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide what changed, the plan or requirements, and a trusted git base and head range.

faq.question: Can it replace human review?
faq.answer: No. It can improve consistency, but critical changes still need appropriate human review.

faq.question: Is there any security concern?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes git shell command examples, so users should run them only with trusted refs in trusted repositories.
